claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have decreased.

result.claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have decreased.
result.reasoning: The claim is strongly supported by evidence from multiple papers. Paper ID 36355784, focusing on Finland, shows a substantial decrease in cervical carcinoma incidence from 15.4 per 10^5 woman-years in the early 1960s to 2.7 per 10^5 woman-years in 1991 (Proposition 3 and 2). This paper attributes the decrease to a nationwide mass screening program. Paper ID 25742130, comparing Finland and the Netherlands, also indicates a decline in incidence rates, stating that "Incidence and mortality rates have declined more in Finland." It provides specific age-adjusted incidence rates for 2003: 4.0 per 100,000 woman-years in Finland (Proposition 7) and 4.9 per 100,000 woman-years in the Netherlands (Proposition 6). Both papers highlight the effectiveness of screening programs in reducing cervical cancer incidence.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted mortality from cervical cancer in Finland was 0.9 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The study compared the incidence of cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: Seven cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the HPV group during the first round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Cervical cancer incidence and mortality data were obtained from the Finnish Cancer Registry.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: HPV-based screening is more effective than cytology in preventing invasive cervical cancer.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: No cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the HPV group during the second round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: In developed countries, the prevalence was estimated to decrease from 1.6% to 0.9%.
evidence.source: Estimates of global prevalence of childhood underweight in 1990 and 2015.

evidence.text: Randomised trials and follow-up of cohort studies consistently demonstrate a significantly lower cumulative incidence of CIN3+ and even of cancer, in women aged 30 years or older, who were at enrollment hrHPV DNA negative compared to those who were cytologically negative.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer has fallen by over 25% since 1992.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: The relative risk of cervical cancer was 0·29 (0·10-0·87; p=0·031).
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer in 1997 was 7% lower than in 1996.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: The prevalence and incidence of onchocerciasis have been substantively reduced.
evidence.source: “Rapid-Impact Interventions”: How a Policy of Integrated Control for Africa's Neglected Tropical Diseases Could Benefit the Poor

evidence.text: The incidence of cervical carcinoma was 2.7 per 10(5) woman-years in 1991.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted mortality from cervical cancer in the Netherlands was 1.4 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted incidence of cervical cancer in the Netherlands was 4.9 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The study compared the mortality rates of cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: At enrollment, the proportion of women in the intervention group who were found to have lesions of grade 2 or 3 cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cancer was 51% greater than the proportion of women in the control group who were found to have such lesions.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus and Papanicolaou tests to screen for cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Cervical cancer was less common in the intervention group (four of 19 579) than in the control group (14 of 19,731).
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted incidence of cervical cancer in Finland was 4.0 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The mean incidence of cervical carcinoma in the early 1960s was 15.4 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.
